content:
# Hydrocarbons

## What is it — Intuitive explanation

Hydrocarbons are **organic compounds made entirely of carbon (C) and hydrogen (H)**. True to their name, they are compounds composed of carbon and hydrogen, and they form the most fundamental group of compounds in the world of organic chemistry.

Carbon atoms have a uniquely special set of properties. Each carbon has four "hands" (bonding arms), and carbon atoms can link to one another to form chains and rings. Depending on the length and shape of these chains, and whether the carbon-carbon bonds are single, double, or triple, hydrocarbons are divided into four major categories: **alkanes** (single bonds only), **alkenes** (containing a double bond), **alkynes** (containing a triple bond), and **aromatic hydrocarbons** (containing a benzene ring).

Hydrocarbons are all around us in everyday life. Methane ($\text{CH}_4$), the main component of natural gas; octane ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$), a key component of gasoline; ethylene ($\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2$), the raw material for plastics; and naphthalene, used in mothballs — all of these are hydrocarbons. Understanding hydrocarbons is essentially like building the core "vocabulary" of all of organic chemistry.

---

## Why it matters — Its place in chemical concepts

Hydrocarbons serve as the **skeleton (backbone)** of organic chemistry. Even the biomolecules that make up our bodies — proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids — can be understood as hydrocarbon skeletons with various functional groups (such as OH and COOH groups) attached to them. In this sense, hydrocarbons are the "starting point" for all organic compounds.

Hydrocarbons also occupy a central place in the modern energy and materials industries. Petroleum and natural gas are mixtures of hydrocarbons, and the various hydrocarbons refined from them serve as raw materials for fuels, plastics, synthetic rubber, and pharmaceuticals. The reactivity of organic compounds — where and how they undergo change — depends heavily on the type of hydrocarbon skeleton involved. Knowing the classification and properties of hydrocarbons is, in effect, like acquiring a map of the entire field of organic chemistry.

---

## The core idea — The essence of definitions and principles

### Classification of hydrocarbons

Hydrocarbons are classified as follows, based on the type of carbon-carbon bonds and overall structure.

**Alkanes — saturated hydrocarbons**

Hydrocarbons in which all carbon-carbon bonds are single bonds ($\text{C-C}$). Their general formula is

$$C_nH_{2n+2} \quad (n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots)$$

"Saturated" means that the carbon atoms are bonded to the maximum possible number of hydrogen atoms. Because only single bonds are present, alkanes are relatively chemically stable and have low reactivity.

**Alkenes — unsaturated hydrocarbons (double bond)**

Hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond ($\text{C=C}$). Their general formula is

$$C_nH_{2n} \quad (n = 2, 3, 4, \ldots)$$

The double bond site is highly reactive and readily undergoes addition reactions (reactions in which the double bond opens and other atoms bond to the carbons).

**Alkynes — unsaturated hydrocarbons (triple bond)**

Hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon triple bond ($\text{C{\equiv}C}$). Their general formula is

$$C_nH_{2n-2} \quad (n = 2, 3, 4, \ldots)$$

The triple bond is even more reactive, and addition reactions can occur in two successive stages.

**Aromatic hydrocarbons**

Hydrocarbons based on the benzene ring ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$) as their fundamental unit. In the benzene ring, six carbon atoms form a regular hexagon, and electrons are delocalized across the entire ring (rather than being fixed to any one bond, they spread throughout the ring). Because of this, aromatic hydrocarbons display a distinctive stability and reactivity quite different from the double bonds found in alkenes.

### Visualizing carbon's bonding hands

Carbon has four bonding hands. In a single bond, it uses one hand at a time to connect with a neighboring atom. In a double bond, it uses two hands together; in a triple bond, three. The more hands used between carbons, the fewer remain available to bond with hydrogen — this is the meaning of "unsaturation."

$$\underset{\text{all hands bonded to H}}{C_nH_{2n+2}} \xrightarrow{\text{introduce one double bond}} \underset{\text{H}_2\text{ is reduced}}{C_nH_{2n}} \xrightarrow{\text{introduce one triple bond}} C_nH_{2n-2}$$

---

## Concrete examples

### Example 1: Methane $\text{CH}_4$ — the main component of natural gas (alkane / everyday life example)

The main component of the natural gas used in kitchen stoves is methane. Methane is the simplest alkane, with one carbon atom bonded to four hydrogen atoms in a tetrahedral arrangement.

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{energy}$$

The most characteristic reaction of alkanes is **combustion**. Because of their stable structure with only single bonds, combustion is the most practically important reaction for alkanes, more so than addition or substitution reactions. Propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$) and butane ($\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$) are also alkanes, used as fuel in portable gas stoves and disposable lighters.

### Example 2: Ethylene $\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2$ — raw material for plastics (alkene)

Ethylene is the simplest example of an alkene, containing one carbon-carbon double bond. The double bond is highly reactive, and ethylene molecules can link together one after another in **addition polymerization**, forming a long chain.

$$n\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2 \rightarrow {(-\text{CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-})}_n$$

The polyethylene (PE) produced by this reaction is widely used in plastic bags and food containers. The ease with which the double bond "opens up" is what underpins the materials industry. Another example of an alkene, propylene ($\text{CH}_3\text{-CH{=}CH}_2$), is the raw material for polypropylene, used in food containers and fibers.

### Example 3: Benzene $\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$ — the archetypal aromatic compound (aromatic hydrocarbon)

Benzene is the archetypal aromatic hydrocarbon, with six carbon atoms arranged in a regular hexagon. At first glance it appears to have three double bonds, but in reality the electrons are spread uniformly around the entire ring (electron delocalization), giving benzene properties entirely different from those of alkenes.

A useful analogy for the benzene ring is a "lake of electrons." Rather than being fixed at one location, the double-bond electrons spread out into a "lake" across the whole ring, giving rise to a special stability known as aromaticity. Because of this stability, benzene favors **substitution reactions** (reactions in which a hydrogen is replaced by another group) over addition reactions. Aromatic hydrocarbons derived from benzene include toluene (used as a solvent), xylene (used in paints), and naphthalene (used in mothballs).

### Example 4: Acetylene $\text{HC{\equiv}CH}$ — the welding flame (alkyne)

Acetylene is the simplest example of an alkyne, containing a triple bond. It produces an extremely high-temperature flame (approximately 3500°C) when burned, making it useful for welding and cutting metals. A defining characteristic of acetylene is that, because of its triple bond, addition reactions can occur in two successive stages — first converting the triple bond to a double bond, then to a single bond.

---

## Connections — Relationships with other concepts

**Prerequisite knowledge: chemical bonding**

Understanding hydrocarbons requires a firm grasp of **covalent bonding**. Single, double, and triple bonds between carbon atoms are all covalent bonds, differing in the number of electrons involved and in bond strength, length, and reactivity. Of the components of a double bond ($\sigma$ bond + $\pi$ bond), the $\pi$ bond is easier to break, and this is what initiates addition reactions.

**Building toward: functional groups and the classification of organic compounds**

When functional groups such as OH, COOH, and NH₂ are added to a hydrocarbon skeleton, a wide variety of organic compounds emerge — alcohols, carboxylic acids, amines, and more. Hydrocarbons are, in effect, the "undecorated skeleton," and the study of functional groups is built directly upon an understanding of that skeleton.

**Building toward: mechanisms of organic reactions**

The characteristic reactions of alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatic compounds — substitution, addition, elimination, and aromatic substitution — form the core of organic reaction mechanisms. Being able to identify which type of hydrocarbon you are dealing with is the essential first step in predicting which reactions will occur.

---

## Applications in other fields

**Energy and environmental science**

Petroleum and natural gas are mixtures of hydrocarbons, and their combustion reactions (the oxidation of alkanes) supply the majority of energy in modern society. At the same time, the $\text{CO}_2$ emissions produced by combustion are a primary driver of climate change, making hydrocarbon chemistry central to research on alternative energy sources and combustion efficiency.

**Materials science and polymer chemistry**

Polymeric materials such as polyethylene, polypropylene, and polystyrene — produced by the addition polymerization of alkenes — are indispensable materials in modern life. Nylon, polyester, and epoxy resins synthesized from aromatic hydrocarbons are used across a broad range of applications, including textiles, electronic components, and aerospace materials.

**Life sciences and pharmaceuticals**

The lipids found in living organisms (which make up cell membranes) have long-chain hydrocarbon skeletons. The molecular structures of many pharmaceuticals, hormones, and vitamins also incorporate hydrocarbon frameworks — particularly aromatic rings and steroid skeletons — and their three-dimensional structures are directly linked to their biological activity. Drug discovery research based on organic chemistry cannot proceed without a foundation in hydrocarbon chemistry.

terms.definition: A general term for organic compounds consisting solely of carbon (C) and hydrogen (H). They are the most fundamental class of compounds in organic chemistry and are classified into alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatic hydrocarbons.
A saturated hydrocarbon in which all bonds between carbon atoms are single bonds (C-C). Represented by the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂, alkanes are relatively chemically stable and have low reactivity.

An unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one double bond (C=C) between carbon atoms. Represented by the general formula CₙH₂ₙ, the double bond site is highly reactive and prone to addition reactions.

An unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one triple bond (C≡C) between carbon atoms. Represented by the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂, alkynes are highly reactive and can undergo addition reactions in two stages.

A hydrocarbon based on the benzene ring (C₆H₆) as its fundamental unit. Electrons are delocalized across the entire ring, giving these compounds a unique stability (aromaticity) and a tendency to undergo substitution reactions.
A hydrocarbon in which all bonds between carbon atoms are single bonds and hydrogen atoms are bonded to the maximum possible extent. Alkanes are the prime example, and they are chemically stable.

A hydrocarbon that contains double or triple bonds between carbon atoms and does not have the maximum number of hydrogen atoms bonded. Alkenes and alkynes fall into this category and are highly reactive.

A reaction in which a double or triple bond breaks open and other atoms or groups become bonded to the carbon atoms. It is a characteristic reaction of alkenes and alkynes, and also forms the basis of addition polymerization.
A reaction in which a specific atom or group in a molecule is replaced by another atom or group. It is a characteristic reaction type of aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene rings) and alkanes.
A state in which electrons are not fixed to a specific bond but are spread over multiple atoms. The delocalization in the benzene ring is the source of the stability associated with aromaticity.

A reaction in which double bonds in alkenes and similar compounds successively open and monomer units link together to form a high-molecular-weight polymer. It is used in the production of polyethylene and polypropylene.

A specific group of atoms (such as OH, COOH, or NH₂) that primarily determines the properties and reactivity of an organic compound. The addition of functional groups to a hydrocarbon skeleton gives rise to a diverse range of organic compounds.
A chemical bond formed when two atoms share electrons. The single, double, and triple bonds between carbon atoms in hydrocarbons are all covalent bonds.

The fundamental structural unit of aromatic hydrocarbons, in which six carbon atoms are arranged in a regular hexagon (C₆H₆). Electron delocalization gives it a special stability known as aromaticity.

A special chemical stability arising from the cyclic delocalization of electrons, as seen in the benzene ring. It is the reason aromatic compounds favor substitution reactions over addition reactions.

<svg viewBox="0 0 600 520" width="600" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">

  <!-- 背景透明 -->

  <!-- タイトル -->
  <text x="300" y="32" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Hydrocarbon Classification Map</text>

  <!-- 中央：炭化水素 -->
  <ellipse cx="300" cy="100" rx="72" ry="30" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="300" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Hydrocarbons</text>
  <text x="300" y="112" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">C and H only</text>

  <!-- 矢印：中央→各分類 -->
  <!-- → アルカン (左上) -->
  <line x1="237" y1="115" x2="115" y2="185" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/>
  <!-- → アルケン (右上) -->
  <line x1="363" y1="115" x2="485" y2="185" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/>
  <!-- → アルキン (左下) -->
  <line x1="260" y1="128" x2="135" y2="290" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/>
  <!-- → 芳香族 (右下) -->
  <line x1="340" y1="128" x2="465" y2="290" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrow)"/>

  <!-- arrowhead marker -->
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="currentColor"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrow-blue" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#3b82f6"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrow-green" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#22c55e"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- ===== アルカン (左上) ===== -->
  <rect x="30" y="185" width="165" height="108" rx="10" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.1" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="112" y="205" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">Alkanes</text>
  <text x="112" y="222" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Single bonds only (C–C)</text>
  <text x="112" y="238" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">General formula: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂</text>
  <text x="112" y="254" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Saturated · Stable · Low reactivity</text>
  <!-- 単結合の図示 -->
  <circle cx="85" cy="277" r="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="91" y1="277" x2="109" y2="277" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="115" cy="277" r="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="121" y1="277" x2="139" y2="277" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="145" cy="277" r="6" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="75" y="274" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">C</text>
  <text x="109" y="274" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">C</text>
  <text x="139" y="274" font-size="9" fill="#3b82f6">C</text>

  <!-- アルカン例 -->
  <rect x="30" y="305" width="165" height="42" rx="6" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="112" y="321" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Ex: Methane CH₄</text>
  <text x="112" y="337" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">City gas · Combustion reaction</text>

  <!-- ===== アルケン (右上) ===== -->
  <rect x="405" y="185" width="165" height="108" rx="10" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.1" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="487" y="205" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">Alkenes</text>
  <text x="487" y="222" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Double bond (C=C)</text>
  <text x="487" y="238" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">General formula: CₙH₂ₙ</text>
  <text x="487" y="254" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Unsaturated · Prone to addition reactions</text>
  <!-- 二重結合の図示 -->
  <circle cx="455" cy="277" r="6" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="461" y1="274" x2="479" y2="274" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="461" y1="280" x2="479" y2="280" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="485" cy="277" r="6" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="445" y="274" font-size="9" fill="#22c55e">C</text>
  <text x="479" y="274" font-size="9" fill="#22c55e">C</text>

  <!-- アルケン例 -->
  <rect x="405" y="305" width="165" height="42" rx="6" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="487" y="321" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Ex: Ethylene CH₂=CH₂</text>
  <text x="487" y="337" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Plastic feedstock · Addition polymerization</text>

  <!-- ===== アルキン (左下) ===== -->
  <rect x="30" y="365" width="165" height="108" rx="10" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.1" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="112" y="385" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#ef4444">Alkynes</text>
  <text x="112" y="402" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Triple bond (C≡C)</text>
  <text x="112" y="418" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">General formula: CₙH₂ₙ₋₂</text>
  <text x="112" y="434" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Highly reactive · 2-step addition reaction</text>
  <!-- 三重結合の図示 -->
  <circle cx="75" cy="455" r="6" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="81" y1="451" x2="99" y2="451" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="81" y1="455" x2="99" y2="455" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/>
  <line x1="81" y1="459" x2="99" y2="459" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="105" cy="455" r="6" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.3" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="65" y="452" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">C</text>
  <text x="99" y="452" font-size="9" fill="#ef4444">C</text>

  <!-- アルキン例 -->
  <rect x="30" y="476" width="165" height="36" rx="6" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="112" y="492" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Ex: Acetylene C₂H₂</text>
  <text x="112" y="506" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Welding gas</text>

  <!-- ===== 芳香族 (右下) ===== -->
  <rect x="405" y="365" width="165" height="108" rx="10" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,2"/>
  <text x="487" y="385" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Aromatic Hydrocarbons</text>
  <text x="487" y="402" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Benzene ring (C₆H₆)</text>
  <text x="487" y="418" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Electron delocalization</text>
  <text x="487" y="434" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Unique stability · Substitution reactions</text>
  <!-- ベンゼン環の図示（六角形） -->
  <polygon points="487,445 500,452 500,464 487,471 474,464 474,452"
    fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <!-- 内円で非局在化を表現 -->
  <circle cx="487" cy="458" r="7" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3,2"/>

  <!-- 芳香族例 -->
  <rect x="405" y="476" width="165" height="36" rx="6" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,2"/>
  <text x="487" y="492" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Ex: Benzene C₆H₆</text>
  <text x="487" y="506" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">Solvent · Synthetic feedstock</text>

  <!-- ===== 結合の手の数（中央下） ===== -->
  <rect x="195" y="185" width="210" height="130" rx="10" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="300" y="204" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Carbon Bonding "Hands"</text>

  <!-- 単結合 -->
  <text x="215" y="225" font-size="11" fill="#3b82f6">● Single bond</text>
  <text x="215" y="240" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">1 bond → max H → saturated</text>

  <!-- 二重結合 -->
  <text x="215" y="258" font-size="11" fill="#22c55e">● Double bond</text>
  <text x="215" y="273" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">2 bonds → H₂ fewer → unsaturated</text>

  <!-- 三重結合 -->
  <text x="215" y="291" font-size="11" fill="#ef4444">● Triple bond</text>
  <text x="215" y="306" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">3 bonds → H₄ fewer → highly reactive</text>

  <!-- 一般式の比較 矢印 -->
  <text x="300" y="355" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ → CₙH₂ₙ → CₙH₂ₙ₋₂</text>
  <text x="300" y="370" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">← More hydrogen (stable)  Reactivity increases →</text>

</svg>

quiz.question: Which of the following correctly matches the general formulas for alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes?
quiz.explanation: Alkanes have only single bonds between carbon atoms and the maximum number of hydrogen atoms, giving $C_nH_{2n+2}$. Alkenes have one double bond, so they have two fewer hydrogen atoms, giving $C_nH_{2n}$. Alkynes have one triple bond, so they have two fewer hydrogen atoms still, giving $C_nH_{2n-2}$. It is useful to remember the pattern that as the number of carbon–carbon bonds increases, the number of hydrogen atoms decreases (i.e., the degree of unsaturation increases).

quiz.question: Benzene ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_6$) also contains carbon–carbon double bonds, yet unlike alkenes it favors substitution reactions over addition reactions. Which of the following best explains the main reason for this?

choices.text: Because benzene has as many as 6 carbon atoms, steric hindrance makes addition reactions difficult.
Because the electrons in the benzene ring are delocalized over the entire ring, giving rise to special stability (aromaticity).
Because benzene carries no hydrogen atoms, there are no positions available for addition.
Because the benzene ring is composed entirely of single bonds and contains no double bonds.

quiz.explanation: In the benzene ring, six carbon atoms form a regular hexagon, and the electrons of the double bonds are not fixed in one location but are spread evenly around the entire ring (electron delocalization). This state, sometimes called a "sea of electrons," produces a special stability known as aromaticity. Because an addition reaction would disrupt this stable delocalized state, benzene preferentially undergoes substitution reactions, in which a hydrogen atom is replaced by another group.

quiz.question: Which of the following correctly pairs the type of reaction by which polyethylene is produced from ethylene ($\text{CH}_2\text{=CH}_2$) with the reason that reaction is possible?

choices.text: Substitution reaction — because it contains a benzene ring, hydrogen atoms are easily replaced by other groups.
Combustion reaction — because alkenes, like alkanes, are stabilized by only single bonds and are most prone to combustion.
Addition polymerization — because the carbon–carbon double bond readily opens, allowing ethylene molecules to bond with one another in succession.
Elimination reaction — because it contains a triple bond, bonds break in a stepwise manner and molecules are eliminated.

quiz.explanation: Ethylene is an alkene containing one carbon–carbon double bond ($\text{C=C}$). The $\pi$ bond within the double bond is relatively easy to break and highly reactive. This property allows ethylene molecules to bond with one another repeatedly through addition polymerization, producing the long-chain polymer polyethylene $\text{(-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-)}_n$. This reaction underpins the manufacture of plastics used in shopping bags, food containers, and many other everyday products.
